paper_title: Towards Robust Deep Learning using Entropic Losses
paper_summary: This thesis tackles the challenging out‑of‑distribution (OOD) detection problem by introducing a family of entropic loss functions and accompanying detection scores that can be plugged directly into existing neural network training pipelines. By leveraging the principle of maximum entropy, the author derives principled objectives that encourage the network to produce calibrated probability distributions while simultaneously making it harder for unseen samples to be misclassified. Extensive experiments demonstrate state‑of‑the‑art OOD detection performance across a variety of benchmark datasets, outperforming many contemporary methods. The proposed losses are computationally lightweight, enabling fast inference, and require no architectural changes or additional post‑processing steps. Beyond OOD, the work also addresses uncertainty estimation, providing more realistic probability outputs that aid in downstream decision making. The combination of theoretical grounding and practical ease of integration makes this contribution a valuable tool for building more trustworthy deep learning systems.
relevance: Reliable deep learning models are essential for safety‑critical applications such as autonomous driving or medical diagnosis. By enabling models to “know when they do not know,” this work reduces the risk of erroneous predictions on unfamiliar data, improving overall system robustness. The entropic loss framework also facilitates better calibrated confidence estimates, which are vital for risk‑aware decision making. Future research can extend these ideas to other domains, such as natural language processing or reinforcement learning, and explore synergistic use with techniques like model ensembles or Bayesian approximations.
